g.data-narration: Concurrency enables multiple tasks to progress simultaneously, maximizing CPU utilization and system responsiveness.
g.text: • Single-tasking OS leaves CPU idle during I/O operations
• Multiprogramming creates illusion of simultaneous execution
• Essential for multi-user systems, web servers, and responsive UIs

g.data-narration: The four pillars of concurrent models are processes, threads, synchronization mechanisms, and threading models.
A process is an executing program with its own address space, containing code, data, stack, and the Process Control Block.
Threads are lightweight execution units within a process that share the same address space, enabling efficient communication.
g.text: Managed by library • Fast • No kernel awareness • One blocks, all block
Managed by OS • Can use multiple CPUs • Higher overhead

g.data-narration: Concurrency manages multiple tasks through rapid switching, while parallelism achieves true simultaneous execution on multi-core CPUs.
g.text: Single CPU switching between tasks • Logical simultaneity
Multiple CPUs executing simultaneously • Physical simultaneity

g.data-narration: The three threading models define how user threads map to kernel threads, affecting parallelism and overhead.
The Many-to-Many model provides the best flexibility, allowing optimal resource utilization across multiple CPUs.
Synchronization primitives like mutexes and semaphores prevent race conditions when multiple threads access shared resources.
The critical section problem requires three conditions: mutual exclusion, progress, and bounded waiting.
g.text: Code segment accessing shared resources that must not be executed by multiple threads simultaneously.
Only one thread in critical section

g.data-narration: The fork() system call creates a child process with a copy of the parent's address space, returning different values to each.
After fork(), parent and child have separate address spaces. Changes in one process do not affect the other.
pthread_create() creates a new thread within a process. Unlike fork(), threads share the same address space.
g.text: pthread_join(thread_id, NULL); // Wait for thread completion
// All threads share: code, data, open files

g.data-narration: Mutexes protect critical sections by ensuring only one thread accesses shared data at a time, preventing race conditions.
Processes provide strong isolation but higher overhead; threads share memory enabling fast communication but require synchronization.
g.text: IPC needed for communication • More isolation

g.data-narration: Concurrent models are essential for modern OS. Key takeaways: understand processes, threads, synchronization, and threading models.
g.text: • Processes = execution units with separate memory
• Threads = CPU execution units sharing address space
• Concurrency = rapid switching | Parallelism = simultaneous execution
• Mutexes & Semaphores ensure data consistency in critical sections
Master these concepts to excel in OS exams and practical programming!
